elements.sent-2411-001: Motivated by known facts about effective field theory and non-Abelian gauge theory, we argue that the post-Newtonian approximation might fail even in the limit of weak fields and small velocities under certain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Namely, the post-Newtonian approximation might break down for wide extended bodies with angular momentum, where angular momentum spans significant spacetime curvature.
elements.sent-2411-003: We construct a novel dimensionless quantity that samples this breakdown, and we evaluate it by means of existing analytical solutions of rotating extended bodies and observational data.
elements.sent-2411-004: We give estimates for galaxies and binary systems, as well as our home in the Cosmos, Laniakea.
elements.sent-2411-005: We thus propose that a novel effective field theory of general relativity is needed to account for the onset of nonlocal angular momentum effetcs, with significant consequences for gravitational physics and cosmology at large.
elements.sent-2411-006: The post-Newtonian approximation of general relativity (GR) is arguably the most successful effective field theory (EFT) in physics \cite{Will_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: The post-Newtonian approximation has been applied both at astronomical, galactic and cosmological scales, with remarkable success throughout all of these scenarios \cite{Clifford_2014,Poisson_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Nonetheless from the galactic scale onward the introduction of non-baryonic dark matter is required for the EFT predictions to match the observations.
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem of dark matter \cite{Bertone_2005,Bekenstein_2004 is a major indicator that `` new physics''\ is required to understand the large scale structure of the universe.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Evidence ranging from disc galaxy rotation curves \cite{Rubin_1978,Bosma_1978,Sofue_2001 , to gravitational lensing events \cite{Treu_2010,Bartelmann_2010 , to the large structure of the universe \cite{DelPopolo_2007 suggests that `` gravity''\, and `` visible matter''\, do not match.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: This requires either a new source of matter or a modification to gravity in the weak-field limit.
elements.sent-2411-012: Yet attempts have been made to suggest that general relativistic effects without Newtonian analogues, when properly accounted for, could explain the astrophysical observations at the galactic level.
elements.sent-2411-013: The proposed analytical solutions for stationary, axisymmetric extended disc galaxies in general relativity \cite{Cooperstock_2007,Balasin_2008,Astesiano_2022a,Astesiano_2022b,Re_2024,Galoppo_2024a,Galoppo_2024b appear to be very far from the post-Newtonian limit.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Whilst the first models of this type were undoubtedly unphysical (e.g., by assuming rigid rotation \cite{Cooperstock_2007,Balasin_2008 ), in recent years these models have shown vast improvement, by introducing differential rotation \cite{Astesiano_2022a,Astesiano_2022b,Re_2024,Galoppo_2024a and even pressure \cite{Galoppo_2024b .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, their net disconnection with any conventional post-Newtonian limit of disc galaxy dynamics has remained.
elements.sent-2411-016: Nonetheless, most of the research community remains sceptical \cite{Rowland_2015,Ciotti_2022,Lasenby_2023,Costa_2024 : the gravitational field of galaxies is extremely weak w.r.t.\ the nonlinearities of GR, and the post-Newtonian expansion has been rigorously developed and tested (see e.g., \cite{Clifford_2014,Poisson_2014,Porto_2016 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: While nonlinear general relativistic effects have been proposed to explain dark energy at cosmological scales \cite{Notari_2006,wiltshire_cosmic_2007-1,Wiltshire_CEP_2008,Buchert_2012 , no convincing general reason has been put forward for why such effects should be present in the case of galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this work, we argue however that the Post-Newtonian expansion could, in fact, fail even for weak gravitational fields and nonrelativistic velocities in a very particular case, i.e., that of extended bodies rotating over scales where the spacetime curvature changes.
elements.sent-2411-019: We note that this situation would then be relevant for galactic and super-galactic scales but not for systems of few bodies, even relativistic ones such as black hole pairs.
elements.sent-2411-020: To build up the argument, we note that whilst effective field theory (EFT) development can be very mathematically sophisticated, its basic principles are relatively simple: one writes down all terms compatible with the fundamental symmetries of the system, and then orders them based on one, or more, small parameters, usually related to scale separation.
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, the post-Newtonian expansion \cite{Clifford_2014,Poisson_2014,Porto_2016 is based on two such scales, i.e., (i) the speed of light is ``large'', w.r.t.\ the typical speeds within the physical system; and (ii) the gravitational field strength is ``weak'', so that nonrelativistic particles cannot be accelerated to relativistic speeds.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The procedures to build EFTs are universal, and yet there are some well-known cases in which the effective expansion fails \cite{Donoghue_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: For such an expansion to make sense, the ``fundamental'', theory must be more symmetric than the EFT for its low-energy limit.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, this is not always the case.
elements.sent-2411-025: When a conservation law present in the effective expansion is violated in the fundamental theory, then the effective expansion is dubious even when a well-defined scale of parameters exists.
elements.sent-2411-026: In quantum physics this becomes inevitable as the representations of the Hilbert space between the two theories are different, but even in classical physics, for periodic solutions, a small violation of a conservation law can build up over many cycles.
elements.sent-2411-027: For a small number of degrees of freedom the Kolmogorov–Arnold–Moser (KAM) theorem guarantees that small perturbations keep perturbations small, but as the number of degrees of freedom goes to infinity this is no longer true \cite{Villani_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Thus, if both anglular momentum and a large number of degrees of freedom are present, weak-field expansions are inherently suspicious.
elements.sent-2411-029: Within field theory, a famous example of the reasoning above is the ``weak-field'', expansion of quantum chromodynamics (QCD).
elements.sent-2411-030: Superficially, it is useful for many applications to expand the chromo-fields around a classical background, $A_\mu \rightarrow A_\mu^{class + \delta A_\mu$, as this approach can produce the correct Feynman rules and $\beta-function$ \cite{Peskin_1995 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, an EFT that expands chromo-fields around a classical background in general will completely miss the fundamental feature of this theory, i.e., quark confinement.
elements.sent-2411-032: The precise reasons for this are not fully understood, but fundamentally, we know that a non-Abelian gauge can be set only locally in the space of field configurations \cite{Gribov_1978,Dudal_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, a `` Post-Maxwellian''\, expansion will be plagued by a multitude of configurations where potentials grow arbitrarily strong, but for which the action still equivalent to a weak expansion of the fundamental theory.
elements.sent-2411-034: Such redundant configurations disappear when one assumes states separated by a certain distance in configuration space (related to the separation of the redundancies in field space) to be colour singlets, which can be thought of as imposing a higher symmetry on the infrared theory than on the fundamental one \cite{Pisarski_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Thus, the correct EFT expansion is not in the basis of fields in QCD, but rather of nonlocal objects represented by the Wilson lines and loops \cite{Pisarski_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The EFT obtained via field expansion only works when the physics considered is ``local enough'' (i.e., when momentum exchanges are large enough that asymptotic freedom, inferred from the beta function \cite{Peskin_1995 , holds).}
elements.sent-2411-037: Note that the issue is not the nonperturbativity of the coupling constant per se, but the highly nonlocal interactions of non-Abelian gauge theory, which becomes an issue when ultraviolet and infrared scales mix.
elements.sent-2411-038: As a demonstration, it is well known \cite{Linde_1980,Arnold_2007 that QCD exhibits infrared divergences for arbitrarily high temperatures, where the `` next neighbour'' coupling constant is arbitrarily small.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The nonperturbative effects ``hide'' in the fact that the thermodynamic limit presupposes a ``large'' volume, and the breakdown of perturbative expansion is then a consequence of this assumption.
elements.sent-2411-040: The nonlocal nature of the problem becomes even more obvious when transport coefficients are calculated, as discussed in \cite{Arnold_2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: This is also true for far-from-equilibrium strongly interacting media, such as in the case of a fast parton exchanging momentum with the medium \cite{Majumder_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Of course, there is no confinement in GR \cite{Deur_2009 as the theory is not defined by a tangent bundle coupling to a vector conserved current.}
elements.sent-2411-043: As such, observables in GR are not gauge invariant and redundancies in asymptotic geometry play a very different role from a spin 1 gauge theory.
elements.sent-2411-044: Nevertheless, some subtleties described above for non-Abelian gauge theories are also present in GR.
elements.sent-2411-045: In GR the ``gauge'' can only be set locally up to small deformations, but it keeps depending on the asymptotic structure of the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-046: While GR is profoundly symmetric, its symmetries are local redundancies rather than global continuous symmetries that generate conservation laws via Noether's theorem \cite{Avery_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: As such, angular momentum is exactly conserved in the classical Newtonian theory and special relativity, but not in GR, where it is crucially sensitive to the asymptotics of frame-dragging effects \cite{Penrose_1982 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: General covariance ensures local conservation of angular momentum in inertial frames, but by its nature it is not necessarily a local quantity, being defined over arbitrarily large distances.
elements.sent-2411-049: The reasoning above makes it clear that it is possible that the post-Newtonian expansion could fail, even when its separation scales naively hold, for a body that (i) it is extended enough that the curvature varies significantly within it's structure; (ii) it carries a significant amount of angular momentum.
elements.sent-2411-050: We notice that this seems exactly to be the case when full GR solutions of extended rotating bodies are considered \cite{Astesiano_2022a,Re_2024,Galoppo_2024b .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Given the discussion above, one can try to define a nonlocal Lorentz-scalar, to function as a dimensionless expansion parameter (according to Buckingham's $\pi$ theorem \cite{buckingham ) which measures the criteria above, namely a special relativistic angular momentum defined over regions of curvature variation.}
elements.sent-2411-052: From dimensional analysis, the lowest-order, non-zero object of this type\footnote{To illustrate this, we could consider a linear parameter in $J^{\alpha \beta \gamma}$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The only non-zero quantity analogous to \eqref{eq:dalpha would be $\order{m^{1 \times J^{\alpha \beta \gamma R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \nu d\Sigma^\nu$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, $J^{\alpha \beta \gamma d\Sigma^\nu$ is not easily relatable to a conserved quantity and there is no natural constant in GR $\sim m$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: These points can only be overcome by a quantity $\order{J \times J}$} is (given a coordinate chart over the spacetime) \begin{widetext \begin{equation}\dd \tilde{\alpha} (x^\mu,y^\mu) := G\, R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta}(x^\mu,y^\mu) J^{\alpha \beta \xi}(x^\mu) J^{\gamma \delta \zeta}(y^\mu) \dd \Sigma_\xi(x^\mu) \dd \Sigma_\zeta (y^\mu) \, , \label{eq:dalpha}\end{equation} \end{widetext where $G$ is the gravitational constant, $R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta}$ is the standard Riemann curvature tensor evaluated along the spatial distance connecting the two spacetime events considered, i.e., $ R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta (x^\mu,y^\mu) := R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta (t, \vec{y -\vec{x )$, $J^{\alpha \beta \gamma}$ is the orbital angular momentum tensor, namely $J^{\mu\nu\sigma := X^{[\mu T^{\nu]\sigma}$, and $\dd\Sigma^\mu$ is the four-vector related to an infinitesimal three-volume element in the spacetime.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, it does have a physical picture associated with it: if one considers two volume elements $\dd \Sigma$ and $\dd \Sigma'$ interacting, in the special relativity the mutual angular momentum is conserved, so that for any closed loop we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:angmomcons} \oint_{\partial \Sigma} J^{\alpha \beta \gamma} \dd \Sigma_\gamma=0 \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: This angular momentum conservation, in extended bodies, leads to vorticity conservation in both extremes of ideal fluid dynamics \cite{Jackiw_2004,Dubovsky_2006 and collisionless kinetic theory \cite{Lynden-Bell_1967 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The special relativistic virial theorem \begin{equation}\label{eq:virial} \underbrace{\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial \dd\Sigma_0^{2}} \int x^{i} x^{j} T^{0 0}\dd \Sigma_\mu}_{\sim V}=2 \underbrace{\int \dd \Sigma_\mu T^{i j}}_{\sim T} \, ,\end{equation} is also proven from the conservation, positiveness and boundedness of $T^{\mu \nu}$ as well as the angular momentum conservation as expressed in Eqn.~\eqref{eq:angmomcons .}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq:virial} \underbrace{\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial \dd\Sigma_0^{2}} \int x^{i} x^{j} T^{0 0}\dd \Sigma_\mu}_{\sim V}=2 \underbrace{\int \dd \Sigma_\mu T^{i j}}_{\sim T} \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, in a curved spacetime, we expect \eqref{eq:angmomcons to be violated, even in freely falling coordinate systems, by an amount $ \sim \dd \tilde{\alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: In particular, considering local conservation and boundedness at infinity of $T^{\mu \nu}$, it is natural from dimensional analysis to assume that for small values of $\alpha$ we would have \begin{equation}\partial_\alpha J^{\alpha \beta \gamma} \sim \order{\tilde{\alpha}} J \, , \ave{T}-2\ave{V} \sim \order{\tilde{\alpha}} \times \order{\ave{T},\ave{V}} \, ,\end{equation} whilst for $\tilde{\alpha \gg 1$ the post-Newtonian expansion would completely break down.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Here, the problem here is not the gravitomagnetic effect per se (which was already addressed in detail in \cite{Rowland_2015,Ciotti_2022,Lasenby_2023,Costa_2024 ) but rather the breaking down of the conservation law of the special relativistic angular momentum together with the nonlocality of its definition.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Indeed, for an extended body this means that the special relativistic angular momentum of every volume element w.r.t.~every other volume element is not conserved.
elements.sent-2411-064: This can then lead to large deviations, even if each pair of volume elements effect is tiny and well described within the post-Newtonian expansion.
elements.sent-2411-065: Characterizing the deviation from the conservation law by the pair of volumes is favoured both by the necessity of an adimensional parameter \cite{buckingham and in analogy with other common physical prescriptions, like the derivation of the Van Der Waals Equation of State \cite{Goodstein_1985 (which also follows the correlation of two volume elements).}}
elements.sent-2411-066: A more precise analogy might be again found in the EFT approach to QCD.
elements.sent-2411-067: Within a medium this is written in terms of Wilson lines, thus introducing important non-locality effects even in ``weak-field'' limits \cite{Wilson_1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: We note that the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter has some similarity to the interaction between two Wilson loops $\ave{W(x) W(x')}$ in QCD.
elements.sent-2411-069: This quantity, i.e., \begin{equation}\ln W= \mathrm{Tr}_a \oint A^{a}_\mu dx^\mu \propto \mathrm{Tr}^a \left[ F^{\mu \nu}F_{\mu \nu}\right]_a \, ,\end{equation} in pure gauge theory interpolates between weakly coupled perimeter law (which becomes a Coloumb potential) and strongly coupled area law (which denotes confinement).
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\ln W= Tr_a \oint A^{a}_\mu dx^\mu \propto Tr^a \left[ F^{\mu \nu}F_{\mu \nu}\right]_a \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: Close to each limit one can develop an expansion around, respectively, weak and strong coupling, but there is no smooth transition from one to the other.
elements.sent-2411-071: Continuing our QCD-driven intuition, the Wilson proof of confinement \cite{Wilson_1974 relies on the ``saturation'' of Wilson loops in an area, which parallels the onset of influence of terms proportional to $\tilde{\alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: This suggests that a large $\tilde{\alpha}$ means that a weak-field expansion is inadequate, and a post-Newtonian theory based on an $\tilde{\alpha}$-expansion, analogous to the effective field theory based on Wilson loops and their correlators \cite{Majumder_2013,Pisarski_2006 , becomes necessary.}
elements.sent-2411-073: While applications of this approach to gravity do exist \cite{white,bonocore they are relevant in the vacuum high energy limit, and a systematic EFT expansion relevant to IR many-body physics is still lacking.}
elements.sent-2411-074: To test the validity of our reasoning, we set out to calculate the value of $\tilde{\alpha}$ for different astrophysical systems of interest, within the conventionally accepted Newtonian approximation of GR.
elements.sent-2411-075: We present an estimate for the value of $\tilde{\alpha}$ for the Alpha-Centauri A and B stellar binary \cite{Wiegert_1997 , a pulsar-neutron star binary system (the Hulse–Taylor pulsar, also identified as PSR B1913+16 \cite{Taylor_1982 )\footnote{We note that, in QCD, we can find the equivalent for a binary system, i.e., a quarkonium state \cite{Brambilla_2000 , where the effective theory is written in terms of a two-body potential.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: We note that if the scalar parameter $\tilde{\alpha}$ correctly traces the onset of nonlocal angular momentum effects within GR, i.e., the breakdown of the post-Newtonian approximation, we expect its value to surpass unity only for the disc galaxy and Laniakea, and not for the other systems.
elements.sent-2411-077: To model the binary systems, given their orbital eccentricity (higher than 0.6) and the almost identical masses of the astronomical bodies \cite{Wiegert_1997,Taylor_1982 , we take the limit of point-like bodies in stationary circular orbits w.r.t.\ their centre of mass.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Moreover, we employ the standard, static Plummer mass profile for the globular cluster \cite{Plummer_1911,Binney_2008 , whilst to model the baryonic component of the disc galaxy we employ a conventional axisymmetric, stationary, razor-thin disc mass distribution, i.e., the Kuzmin profile \cite{Kuzmin_1956,Binney_2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, to model Laniakea, we consider its most recent determination \cite{Dupuy_2023 , which employs the velocity and density field reconstruction of \cite{Courtois_2023 , derived from the latest Cosmicflows-4 (CF4) data release \cite{Tully_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus, in our work Laniakea is represented by 4079 voxels within a grid of 128$^3$ cells covering an overall volume of 1 (Gpc/$h)^3$, where $h$ is the reduced Hubble constant.
elements.sent-2411-081: To estimate $\tilde{\alpha}$, we consider the conventional Newtonian line element of GR in cylindrical coordinates for all the systems but Laniakea, for which it is convenient to use standard Cartesian coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:Nmetric} \dd s^2 = -\left(1-\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)c^2\dd t^2 + \left(1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)\left(\dd r^2 + \dd z^2\right) \nonumber + \left(1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)r^2\dd\phi^2\, ,\end{equation} where $\Phi(t,r,z,\phi)$ is the Newtonian potential.
elements.sent-2411-083: The geometry of Eqn.~\eqref{eq:Nmetric is then coupled to the energy-momentum tensor $T^{\mu\nu}$ of these systems.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The orbiting binaries have zero internal pressure, a constant orbital rotational velocity, i.e. $\Omega(r,z) = \Omega_0$, and a density profile given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:dens_bin} \rho(r,z,\phi) = \frac{\bar{M}}{r}\delta(z)\delta(r-r_{0})\left[\delta(\phi - \Omega_0 t) + \delta(\phi - \Omega_0 t + \pi)\right] \, ,\end{equation} where $\bar{M}$ is the average mass of the two bodies in the system, $\delta$ is the Dirac delta distribution, $\Omega_0$ is their orbital rotational speed and $r_{0}$ is the radius of the orbit.
elements.sent-2411-086: We take $\bar{M = 1\,\text{M _\odot$ and $\bar{M = 1.4\,\text{M _\odot$ for the stellar binary and the Hulse–Taylor pulsar, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, we have $\Omega_0 = 2.5 \cdot 10^{-9}\, \text{rad/s}$ and $\Omega_0 = 2.25 \cdot 10^{-4}\, \text{rad/s}$, whilst $r_{0 = 24\, \text{AU}$ and $r_{0 = 0.01\, \text{AU}$ for the two systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: For the stellar globular cluster, we are considering a static effective fluid description derived from a time-average over the chaotic orbits of the stars within the globular cluster, such that $\Omega(r,z) = 0$, and the density is given by \cite{Plummer_1911,Binney_2008 \begin{equation}\rho(r,z) = \frac{3\,\Rb^2\Mb}{4\pi\left(r^2+z^2+\Rb^2\right)^{5/2}} \,,\end{equation} where $\Rb$ is the core radius of the globular cluster, so that the whole cluster should be contained within two to three such radii, and $\Mb$ is its total mass.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In our estimates, we model a massive globular cluster, and we thus take the realistic values $\Rb = 30 $ pc and $\Mb = 10^6\,\text{M _\odot$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: For the disc galaxy, the density profile is given by the Kuzmin profile \cite{Kuzmin_1956,Binney_2008 , i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:rdensity} \rho(r,z)= \frac{\Md \rd}{2\pi\left(r^2 + \rd^2\right)^{3/2}}\delta(z)\, ,\end{equation} where $\Md$ is the total disc mass and $\rd$ is the scale length in the radial direction for the galactic disc.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The rotational speed field of the fluid elements for the disc galaxy model is then given by \begin{equation}\Omega(r,0) = \sqrt{\frac{G\Md}{\left(r^2+\rd^2\right)^{3/2}}}\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-092: We can also directly define the Newtonian potentials for the two extended matter configurations with an analytical description for their densities (we note that for the binary systems, this is just the superposition of the potential generated by two point-like sources).
elements.sent-2411-093: For the globular cluster we have \begin{equation}\Phi(r,z) = -\frac{G\Mb}{\sqrt{r^2+z^2+\Rb^2}}\,,\end{equation} and for the disc galaxy \begin{equation}\Phi(r,z) = -\frac{G\Md}{\sqrt{r^2+(|z|+\rd)^2}}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: Furthermore, the pressure can be obtained numerically by solving for hydrostatic equilibrium in these systems \cite{Binney_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: On the other hand, the energy-momentum tensor for Laniakea is directly obtained from the three-dimensional density and velocity field reconstructions \cite{Courtois_2023,Dupuy_2023 from CF4 \cite{Tully_2023 , whilst the Newtonian potential is numerically derived from the superposition of the Newtonian potential generated by each voxel in the reconstruction.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: In this paper, we consider Laniakea as an isolated system w.r.t.\ to the rest of the Universe, so we fix the density field outside Laniakea to be zero.
elements.sent-2411-097: We can now calculate the parameter $\tilde{\alpha}$ for these systems.
elements.sent-2411-098: As the full expression is extremely, complicated, we can consider the leading term in powers of $\epsilon := v/c$, where $v$ is the typical nonrelativistic velocity in these systems.
elements.sent-2411-099: We note that under the Newtonian approximation, we also have $\Phi/c^2 \goesas \epsilon^2$.
elements.sent-2411-100: We find \begin{widetext \begin{equation}\tilde{\alpha} = \frac{4G}{c^2}\int_0^\pi f(\Theta) \dd\Theta \int\int r^2 r'^2\rho(r,0)\rho(r',0)\partial^2_r\Phi(|r-r'|,0)\dd r \dd r' \, + \order{\epsilon^4},\end{equation} \end{widetext where $f(\Theta) = 1$ for the globular cluster, given the spherical symmetry of the system, and $f(\Theta) = \delta(\Theta)$ for the other astronomical objects.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Furthermore, as we use observational data on a three-dimensional grid to describe Laniakea, Eqn.~\eqref{eq:myalpha is therefore discretised and numerically calculated on the underlying finite size of the grid.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Note that relativistic angular momentum has a component driven by gradients of the potential, which can dominate for large gradients and nonrelativistic velocities.
elements.sent-2411-103: For instance, in the case of globular clusters, the fact that the shear component of the gradient is small will lead to a small value of $\tilde{\alpha}$, a value that will not change much when velocity dispersion is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-104: Moreover, we point out that given the reasoning used in the definition of $\tilde{\alpha}$ in terms of vorticity conservation and virial theorem, $G \times M_\odot^2$ is the natural scale to measure $\tilde{\alpha}$ since it matches the scale of the ``atoms'' (i.e. stars) making up the astrophysical systems under considerations.
elements.sent-2411-105: The results are summarised in Table \ref{tab:1.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \caption{Estimates for the values of $\tilde{\alpha}$ for the astrophysical systems considered.} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|} Estimates for the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter Stellar binary $10^{-9}$ Pulsar binary $10^{-5}$ Globular Cluster $10^{-4}$ Disc galaxy $10^{16}$ Laniakea $10^{36}$ \end{tabular} \label{tab:1}
elements.sent-2411-106: We find that the results in Table \ref{tab:1 are in line with the expectations following our reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The novel parameter we propose takes fairly different values whether we consider isolated binaries or continuous, spinning bodies.
elements.sent-2411-108: In particular, the values for $\tilde{\alpha}$ are well below unity in systems which do not require any DM and are well described by the post-Newtonian approximation of GR.
elements.sent-2411-109: In contrast, for wide-extended bodies with a considerable angular momentum over a scale where the curvature varies, such as a disc galaxy or Laniakea, the obtained value is orders of magnitudes above unity.
elements.sent-2411-110: This could then signal a break-down of the post-Newtonian approximation, and thus the rising necessity for a novel effective field theory appropriate for $\tilde{\alpha \gg 1$, perhaps by using approaches such as \cite{white,bonocore .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Our considerations in this paper are very generic, anticipating the future construction of a novel EFT of GR.
elements.sent-2411-112: They should apply to any situation where there is non-negligible angular momentum (defined in special relativistic terms) over scales ``large'' compared to the curvature.
elements.sent-2411-113: However, at the moment we do not have an effective theory in $\tilde{\alpha}$ which can be used to actually calculate corrections to the post-Newtonian expansion.
elements.sent-2411-114: Nonetheless, the linearised quasilocal angular momentum corrections in \cite{Galoppo_2024b are reasonably expected to arise from such an effective theory.}
elements.sent-2411-115: We can however say something about where such effective theory corrections become necessary.
elements.sent-2411-116: The first obvious application are disc galaxy rotation curves.
elements.sent-2411-117: The second direct application is in the dynamics of galaxy clusters, given the value of the parameter obtained for $\tilde{\alpha}$ for Laniakea.
elements.sent-2411-118: In particular, the Bullet cluster \cite{Clowe_2004 , thought to be a direct and straight-forward signature of DM, also fits in the range of validity of the approach here, since it is a collision with significant velocities and an impact parameter comparable to the radius of curvature of the galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The calculations here do not touch the issue of light-bending in relativity, but it is reasonable that a nonlocal expansion around a scale such as $\tilde{\alpha}$ could yield significant corrections to the lensing observables used to characterize \cite{Clowe_2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-120: One piece of evidence of dark matter where angular momentum seems to play no role is based on the anisotropies of the CMB \cite{Planck_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: However, if the evidence of Magnetic fields on such scales \cite{Matarrese_2005,Durrer_2013 is vindicated, independently of their origin, a momentum associated to them is inevitable, and a non-local general relativistic effect arising from this angular momentum could be non-negligible.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Moreover, the pioneering work by Bruni and collaborators \cite{Bruni_2013,Thomas_2015 gives the first tantalizing evidence of the importance that angular momentum and frame-dragging might play even in cosmology at the level of structure formation.}
elements.sent-2411-123: In conclusion, driven by analogies with non-Abelian gauge theory we have argued that post-Newtonian effective field theory should fail if the adimensional constant $\tilde{\alpha}$, which we constructed, is non-negligible.
elements.sent-2411-124: We have estimated $\tilde{\alpha}$ for a variety of astronomical objects, and found that it is small for systems well described by the post-Newtonian approximation and with no signs of dark matter, while it is large for disc galaxies and Laniakea, where dark matter is thought to play an important role.
elements.sent-2411-125: We point out that potentially $\tilde{\alpha}$ could be non-negligible for other setups, such as the bullet cluster and cosmological scales, where dark matter dominates the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-126: Therefore, the future development of a nonlinear many-body effective theory of general relativity suitable for large $\tilde{\alpha}$ represent an exciting challenge, which might carry heavy consequences for gravitational physics and cosmology at large.. \quad G.T.
elements.sent-2411-127: The authors are deeply grateful to Robert Brandenberger, Paul Chouha, Räsänen Syksy, and Federico Re for their insight and our fruitful discussions via correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-128: We are deeply thankful to Riccardo Sturani for his clear comments and the opportunity of presenting this work at the ICTP-SAIFR in Sao Paulo.
elements.sent-2411-129: We sincerely thank Zachary Lane for his irreplaceable expertise in dealing with observational data.
elements.sent-2411-130: Finally, we thank Christopher Harvey-Hawes, Morag Hills, Emma Johnson, Shreyas Tiruvaskar, Michael Williams and David Wiltshire for useful discussions.
elements.sent-2411-131: Motivated by known facts about effective field theory and non-Abelian gauge theory, we argue that the post-Newtonian approximation might fail even in the limit of weak fields and small velocities for wide-extended rotating bodies, where angular momentum spans significant spacetime curvature.
elements.sent-2411-132: We thus propose that a novel effective field theory of general relativity might be needed to account for the onset of nonlocal angular momentum effects.
elements.sent-2411-133: The problem of dark matter \cite{Bertone_2005,Bekenstein_2004 is a major indicator that ``new physics''\ is required to understand the large scale structure of the universe.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Evidence ranging from disc galaxy rotation curves \cite{Rubin_1978,Bosma_1978,Sofue_2001 , to gravitational lensing events \cite{Treu_2010,Bartelmann_2010 , to the large structure of the universe \cite{DelPopolo_2007 suggests that ``gravity''\, and ``visible matter''\, do not match.}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The proposed analytical solutions for stationary, axisymmetric extended disc galaxies in general relativity \cite{Cooperstock_2007,Balasin_2008,Astesiano_2022a,Astesiano_2022b,Ruggiero_2024,Re_2024,Galoppo_2024b appear to be very far from the post-Newtonian limit.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Whilst the first models of this type were undoubtedly unphysical (e.g., by assuming rigid rotation \cite{Cooperstock_2007,Balasin_2008 ), in recent years these models have shown vast improvement, by introducing differential rotation \cite{Astesiano_2022a,Astesiano_2022b,Ruggiero_2024,Re_2024 and even pressure \cite{Galoppo_2024b .}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: This has made most of the research community sceptical about the physical relevance of such models \cite{Rowland_2015,Ciotti_2022,Lasenby_2023,Costa_2024 : the gravitational field of galaxies is extremely weak w.r.t.\ the nonlinearities of GR, and the post-Newtonian expansion has been rigorously developed and tested (see e.g., \cite{Clifford_2014,Poisson_2014,Porto_2016 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-138: While nonlinear general relativistic effects have been proposed to explain dark energy at cosmological scales \cite{Notari_2006,Wiltshire_cosmic_2007-1,Wiltshire_CEP_2008,Buchert_2012 , no convincing general reason has been put forward for why such effects should be present in the case of galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Then, these two scales are conventionally put together via a single expansion parameter, which relates the compactness of the system to the orbital velocities within the system.
elements.sent-2411-140: For a small number of degrees of freedom the Kolmogorov–Arnold–Moser (KAM) theorem guarantees that perturbations to a system dynamics due to symmetry breaking terms remain negligible \cite{Goldstein_2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-141: However, as the number of degrees of freedom goes to infinity this is no longer true \cite{Villani_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: Thus, if both angular momentum and a large number of degrees of freedom are present, weak-field expansions are inherently suspicious.
elements.sent-2411-143: Superficially, it is useful for many applications to expand the chromo-fields around a classical background, $A_\mu \rightarrow A_\mu^{\mathrm{classic + \delta A_\mu$, as this approach can produce the correct Feynman rules and $\beta-$function \cite{Peskin_1995 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Therefore, a ``Post-Maxwellian''\, expansion will be plagued by a multitude of configurations where potentials grow arbitrarily strong, but for which the action is still equivalent to a weak expansion of the fundamental theory.
elements.sent-2411-145: Thus, the correct EFT expansion is not on the basis of fields in QCD, but rather of nonlocal objects represented by the Wilson lines and loops \cite{Pisarski_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-146: The EFT obtained via field expansion only works when the physics considered is ``local enough'' (i.e., when momentum exchanges are large enough that asymptotic freedom applies, inferred from the beta function \cite{Peskin_1995 , or when the only distance scale is a correspondingly small one, such as in the case of Quarkonium \cite{Brambilla_2000 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Note that while this is usually thought of as a ``quantum effect", the real issue is the non-existence of a real ``semiclassical limit" where small perturbations, classical or quantum, are firmly divided from the background.
elements.sent-2411-148: Here, the issue is not the nonperturbativity of the coupling constant per se, but the highly nonlocal interactions of non-Abelian gauge theory, which becomes an issue when ultraviolet and infrared scales mix.
elements.sent-2411-149: As such, angular momentum is exactly conserved in the classical Newtonian theory and special relativity, but not in GR.
elements.sent-2411-150: Within GR, angular momentum is crucially sensitive to the asymptotics of frame-dragging effects, and, in a general spacetime, it's not globally conserved due to curvature gradients \cite{Penrose_1982 .}
elements.sent-2411-151: General covariance ensures local conservation of angular momentum in inertial frames, but by its nature it is not a local quantity, being defined over arbitrarily large distances w.r.t.\ a reference point.
elements.sent-2411-152: The reasoning above makes it clear that it is possible that the post-Newtonian expansion could fail, even when its separation scales naively hold, for a body that (i) is extended enough that the curvature varies significantly within its structure; (ii) carries a significant amount of angular momentum.
elements.sent-2411-153: Given the discussion above, one can try to define a nonlocal Lorentz-scalar, to function as a dimensionless expansion parameter (according to Buckingham's $\pi$ theorem \cite{buckingham ) which quantifies the criteria above, namely a special relativistic angular momentum defined over regions of curvature variation.}
elements.sent-2411-154: It is then convenient to work within a foliation framework, with a 3 + 1 splitting of a general spacetime \cite{Gourgoulhon_2012 , and define a Lorentz scalar ultimately adapted to the spatial hypersurfaces of the foliation.}
elements.sent-2411-155: These points can only be overcome by a quantity $\order{J \times J}$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Here, $G$ is the gravitational constant, $J^{\alpha \beta \gamma}$ is the orbital angular momentum tensor, $J^{\mu\nu\sigma := X^{[\mu T^{\nu]\sigma}$, and $\dd\Sigma^\mu$ is the timelike four-vector related to an infinitesimal three-volume element in the spacetime.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Moreover, we have defined the interaction kernel as a bi-tensor \cite{Synge_1960,Poisson_2011 via \begin{equation}R_{\alpha' \beta' \gamma \delta}(x^\mu,y^\mu):= \frac{1}{2}\left[\mathcal{P}_{\alpha '\xi}(x^\mu,y^\mu)\mathcal{P}_{\beta '\zeta}(x^\mu,y^\mu)R^{\xi \zeta}_{\phantom{\xi} \phantom{\zeta} \gamma \delta}\left(y^\mu\right)+\mathcal{P}_{\gamma \xi'}(x^\mu,y^\mu)\mathcal{P}_{\delta \zeta '}(x^\mu,y^\mu)R_{\alpha ' \beta '}^{\phantom{\alpha '} \phantom{\beta '} \xi' \zeta'}\left(x^\mu\right)\right]\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Here, $\mathcal{P _{\alpha'\beta (x^\mu,y^\mu)$ is the canonical parallel transport bi-tensor from $(t;\vec{x )$ to $(t;\vec{y )$ along the shortest geodesic within the spatial hypersurface \cite{Poisson_2011}\footnote{We note that not for all spacetimes such geodesic is unique.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: When necessary, a direct generalisation of $\mathcal{P _{\alpha'\beta (x^\mu,y^\mu)$ is obtained via direct averages of the parallel transport along all such minimal geodesics.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Then, by direct double integration of $\dd \tilde{\alpha}$ on a spacelike foliation slice we obtain \begin{equation}\label{eq:myalpha} \tilde{\alpha} = G\iint R_{\mu'\nu'\rho\sigma}(x^\mu,y^\mu)J^{\mu'\nu'\beta'}(x^\mu)J^{\sigma\rho\gamma}(y^\mu)\dd\Sigma_{\beta'}(x^\mu)\dd\Sigma_{\gamma}(y^\mu)\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-161: This novel expansion parameter, although covariantly defined, depends on the choice of foliation\footnote{We also remark that in principle the expansion parameter in the post-Newtonian EFT is not only foliation-dependent but also, non-covariantly defined.}
elements.sent-2411-162: However, it can be made covariant by employing the spatial projection on the chosen foliation to define the spatial velocity of the matter.. .
elements.sent-2411-163: Such a situation is not quite unique to this new quantity, as almost every scalar out of such a spatial integration would be foliation dependent \cite{Mourier_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: In general, $\tilde{\alpha}$ is not a directly observable physical quantity (as it mixes special relativistic angular momentum and curvature) but is rather an expansion parameter, tracking the applicability of post-Newtonian EFT\footnote{The expression for the interaction kernel $R_{\alpha' \beta' \gamma \delta (x^\mu,y^\mu)$ reduces to $\left[R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta (t; \vec{x ) + R_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta (t; \vec{y )\right]/2$ at first order within the post-Newtonian EFT, symplifying the calcualtion of the expansion parameter.. .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: However, it can be roughly evaluated for astrophysical systems via observables that allow for estimates of their global curvature, $\ave{R}$, (e.g., detailed mass modelling and gravitational lensing), and high-resolution observations capable of estimating the volume, $\ave{V}$ (related to the radius of the luminosity distribution), and the special relativistic angular momentum, $\ave{J}$ (e.g., via velocities reconstructions).
elements.sent-2411-166: Using a rough order of magnitude estimate, we can then obtain an approximated scaling observable for physical systems, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{scaling} \frac{\tilde{\alpha}}{G} \sim \ave{R}\times \ave{J}^{2} \times \ave{V}^{2} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-167: Furthermore, the novel expansion parameter $ \tilde{\alpha}$ does have a physical picture associated with it: if one considers two fluid volume elements $\dd \Sigma$ and $\dd \Sigma'$ interacting, in special relativity the mutual angular momentum is conserved, so that for any closed loop we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:angmomcons} \oint_{\partial \Sigma} J^{\alpha \beta \gamma} \dd \Sigma_\gamma=0 \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: The special relativistic virial theorem \cite{Schutz_1985 expressed in the foliation formalism \begin{equation}\label{eq:virial} \underbrace{\frac{\partial^{2}}{\partial \Sigma_0^{2}} \int \Sigma^{i} \Sigma^{j} T^{0 0}{\text{d}\Sigma_0} }_{\sim V}=2 \underbrace{\int T^{i j} {\text{d}\Sigma_0}}_{\sim T} \, ,\end{equation} is also proven from the conservation, positiveness and boundedness of $T^{\mu \nu}$ as well as the angular momentum conservation as expressed in Eqn.~\eqref{eq:angmomcons .}}
elements.sent-2411-169: In particular, considering local conservation and boundedness at infinity of $T^{\mu \nu}$, it is natural from dimensional analysis to assume that for small values of $\tilde{\alpha}$ we would have \begin{equation}\partial_{\mu} J^{\mu \nu \rho} \sim \order{\tilde{\alpha}^{1/2}} J \, , \ave{T}-2\ave{V} \sim \order{\tilde{\alpha}^{1/2}} \times \order{\ave{T},\ave{V}} \, ,\end{equation} whilst for $\tilde{\alpha \gg 1$ the post-Newtonian expansion would completely break down.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Here, the problem is not the gravitomagnetic effect per se (which was already addressed in detail in \cite{Rowland_2015,Ciotti_2022,Lasenby_2023,Costa_2024 ) but rather the breaking down of the conservation law of the special relativistic angular momentum together with the nonlocality of its definition.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Indeed, for an extended body this means that the special relativistic angular momentum of every volume element w.r.t.\ every other volume element is not conserved.
elements.sent-2411-172: We note that the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter has some similarity to the interaction between two Wilson loops $\ave{W(x) W(x')}$ in QCD. \begin{equation}\ln W= \mathrm{Tr}_a \oint A^{a}_\mu dx^\mu \propto \mathrm{Tr}^a \left[ F^{\mu \nu}F_{\mu \nu}\right]_a \, ,\end{equation} in pure gauge theory this quantity has two scalings w.r.t.\ the coupling constant: weakly coupled perimeter law (which becomes a Coloumb potential) and strongly coupled area law (which denotes confinement) \cite{Greensite_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-173: While applications of this approach to gravity do exist \cite{White_2011,Bonocore_2022 , they are relevant in the vacuum high energy limit, and a systematic EFT expansion relevant to IR many-body physics is still lacking.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Perhaps joining the techniques of \cite{White_2011,Bonocore_2022 with the Wilson line effective theory which was developed in the context of extended bodies in QCD \cite{Majumder_2013,Pisarski_2006 would yield an effective expansion in terms of $\tilde{\alpha}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-175: To test the validity of our reasoning, we set out to calculate the value of $\tilde{\alpha}$ for different astrophysical systems of interest, within the conventionally accepted Newtonian approximation of GR\footnote{We note that employing the Newtonian approximation to estimate $\tilde{\alpha}$ does not constitute a contradiction w.r.t.}
elements.sent-2411-176: It is the dynamic of the system which might not then be well approximated by the post-Newtonian expansion when $\tilde{\alpha}$ calculated this way is large.. .
elements.sent-2411-177: We note that if the scalar parameter $\tilde{\alpha}$ correctly traces the onset of nonlocal angular momentum effects within GR, i.e., the possible breakdown of the post-Newtonian approximation, its value to must be much smaller than unity for all binary systems, where the post-Newtonian approximation has been directly tested \cite{Poisson_2014,Clifford_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-178: To model the binary systems \cite{Wiegert_1997,Taylor_1982 , we take the limit of point-like bodies\footnote{To avoid divergence in the Newtonian potential we employ within the astronomical objects the effective gravitational potential $\Phi(r) = -GM\left(3r_b^2-r^2\right)/2r_{b ^3$, where $r_b$ represents the radius of the objects, much smaller than the typical orbital radius.}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: Such a choice can be understood in a rough modelling of the astronomical bodies as spheres of uniform densities.
elements.sent-2411-180: Moreover, we employ the standard, static Plummer mass profile for the globular cluster \cite{Plummer_1911,Binney_2008 , whilst to model the baryonic component of the disc galaxies we employ a conventional axisymmetric, stationary, razor-thin disc mass distribution, i.e., the Kuzmin profile \cite{Kuzmin_1956,Binney_2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Finally, to model Laniakea, we consider its recent determination \cite{Dupuy_2023}\footnote{In this paper, we do not employ the most recent determination of Laniakea \cite{Valade_2024 as no velocity field coupled to a density field reconstruction is available at the moment.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Thus, in our paper Laniakea is represented by 4079 voxels within a grid of 128$^3$ cells covering an overall volume of 1 (Gpc/$h)^3$, where $h$ is the reduced Hubble constant.
elements.sent-2411-183: Thus, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:Nmetric} \dd s^2 = -\left(1-\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)c^2\dd t^2 + \left(1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)\left(\dd r^2 + \dd z^2\right) + \left(1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}\right)r^2\dd\phi^2\, ,\end{equation} where $\Phi(t,r,z,\phi)$ is the Newtonian potential.
elements.sent-2411-185: The binary systems, being only rotationally supported, are taken with a zero internal pressure, a constant orbital rotational velocity, i.e. $\Omega(r,z) = \Omega_0$, and a density profile given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:dens_bin} \rho(t,r,z,\phi) = \frac{\bar{M}}{r}\delta(z)\delta(r-r_{0})\left[\delta(\phi - \Omega_0 t) + \delta(\phi - \Omega_0 t + \pi)\right] \, ,\end{equation} where $\bar{M}$ is the average mass of the two bodies in the system, $\delta$ is the Dirac delta distribution, $\Omega_0$ is their orbital rotational speed and $r_{0}$ is the radius of the orbit.
elements.sent-2411-186: Moreover, we have $\Omega_0 = 2.5 \cdot 10^{-9}\, \text{rad/s}$ and $\Omega_0 = 2.25 \cdot 10^{-4}\, \text{rad/s}$, respectively, whilst $r_{0 = 24\, \text{AU}$ and $r_{0 = 0.01\, \text{AU}$, respecitvely, for the two systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: For the stellar globular cluster, we are considering a static effective fluid description derived from a time-average over the chaotic orbits of the stars within the globular cluster, such that $\Omega(r,z) = 0$, and the density is given by spherically symmetric distribution \cite{Plummer_1911,Binney_2008 \begin{equation}\rho(r,z) = \frac{3\,\Rb^2\Mb}{4\pi\left(r^2+z^2+\Rb^2\right)^{5/2}} \,,\end{equation} where $\Rb$ is the core radius of the globular cluster, so that the whole cluster should be contained within two to three such radii, and $\Mb$ is its total mass.}
elements.sent-2411-188: For both disc galaxies, the density profile is given by the Kuzmin profile \cite{Kuzmin_1956,Binney_2008 , i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:rdensity} \rho(r,z)= \frac{\Md \rd}{2\pi\left(r^2 + \rd^2\right)^{3/2}}\delta(z)\, ,\end{equation} where $\Md$ is the total disc mass and $\rd$ is the scale length in the radial direction for the galactic disc.}
elements.sent-2411-189: We take $\Md = 10^{6 \,\mathrm{M _\odot$, $\Md = 10^{10 \,\mathrm{M _\odot$, and $\rd = 0.5 \,\mathrm{kpc}$, $\rd = 2.8 \,\mathrm{kpc}$, respectively, for the dwarf disc galaxy and the Milky Way-like disc galaxy.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The angular velocity field of the fluid elements for the disc galaxy model is then given by \begin{equation}\Omega(r,z) = \delta(z)\sqrt{\frac{G\Md}{\left(r^2+\rd^2\right)^{3/2}}}\, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-191: For the globular cluster we have \begin{equation}\Phi(r,z) = -\frac{G\Mb}{\sqrt{r^2+z^2+\Rb^2}}\,,\end{equation} and for the disc galaxies \begin{equation}\Phi(r,z) = -\frac{G\Md}{\sqrt{r^2+(|z|+\rd)^2}}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-192: To estimate the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter, we consider Laniakea as an isolated system w.r.t.\ to the rest of the Universe, so we fix the density field outside Laniakea to be zero.
elements.sent-2411-193: Therefore, following the same procedure as in \cite{Giani_2024 , we subtract from the reconstructed velocity field the Hubble flow to offset the effects of any external gravitational field.}
elements.sent-2411-194: We can now calculate the parameter $\tilde{\alpha}$ for these systems at leading order in powers of $\epsilon := v/c$, where $v$ is the typical nonrelativistic velocity in these systems.
elements.sent-2411-195: We find\footnote{We have submitted our Mathematica code, \lq\lq Nonlocal\_Expansion\_GR'' as an ancillary file.}
elements.sent-2411-196: The code defines the spacetime metric at first order in the post-Newtonian formalism, and calculates the relative energy-momentum tensor in cylindrical coordinates for the matter source.
elements.sent-2411-197: Finally, it obtains at first order in the expansion the formula for $\tilde{\alpha}$, i.e., Eq.\ \eqref{eq:alpha_final . \begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha_final} \tilde{\alpha} \simeq -\frac{16\pi^2 G}{c^2}\left(\iiiint \left[r\rho(r,z)r'\rho(r',z')\left(rr'\partial^2_r\Phi(r,z) + zz'\partial^2_z\Phi(r,z)\right) \right] \dd r \dd r'\dd z \dd z' \right. +\left. \iiiint r\rho(r,z)r'\rho(r',z') \left[rz' + zr'\right]\partial_r\partial_z\Phi(r,z) \dd r \dd r'\dd z \dd z' \right)\, ,\nonumber\end{equation} where for the binary systems the $\phi$ dependence has already been integrated out, thus defining an effective $\rho(r,z)$ for such systems.}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\label{eq:alpha_final} \tilde{\alpha} \simeq -\frac{16\pi^2 G}{c^2}\left(\iiiint \left[r\rho(r,z)r'\rho(r',z')\left(rr'\partial^2_r\Phi(r,z) + zz'\partial^2_z\Phi(r,z)\right) \right] \dd r \dd r'\dd z \dd z' \right. +\left. \iiiint r\rho(r,z)r'\rho(r',z') \left[rz' + zr'\right]\partial_r\partial_z\Phi(r,z) \dd r \dd r'\dd z \dd z' \right)\, ,\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-198: For Laniakea, since we employ observational data on a three-dimensional grid, Eq. \eqref{eq:myalpha is discretised and evaluated at the leading order.}
elements.sent-2411-199: The results are summarised in Table \ref{tab:1 .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \caption{Estimates for the values of $\tilde{\alpha}$ for the astrophysical systems considered.} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|} Estimate for the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter Stellar binary $10^{-9}$ Pulsar binary $10^{-5}$ Globular Cluster $10^{-4}$ Dwarf disc galaxy $10^{4}$ Massive disc galaxy $10^{13}$ Laniakea $10^{26}$ \end{tabular} \label{tab:1}
elements.sent-2411-200: The values for $\tilde{\alpha}$ are well below unity in systems for which the post-Newtonian has been directly verified.
elements.sent-2411-201: Moreover, the same is true for the globular cluster, in which the static assumption -- which relates to the isotropic average zero velocity of the effective fluid element in such systems -- implies that only the exclusively special relativistic components of the angular momentum contribute to the estimate of $\tilde{\alpha}$, giving overall a small contribution.
elements.sent-2411-202: In this regard, the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter spans eight orders of magnitude between the massive globular cluster and the dwarf disc galaxy, having the same total mass.
elements.sent-2411-203: Such a difference cannot be reconciled with simply a different spatial extension of the two systems, as the characteristic radii give a ratio $\Rb/\rd \approx 0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-204: Instead, it signals a direct difference in the overall degrees of freedom in the angular momentum distributions of the two systems.
elements.sent-2411-205: As argued in this paper, a value for the $\tilde{\alpha}$ parameter above unity could then signal a break-down of the post-Newtonian approximation -- whose applicability is no longer guaranteed by the KAM theorem \cite{Villani_2011 -- and thus the necessity for a novel EFT appropriate for $\tilde{\alpha \gg 1$, perhaps by using approaches such as \cite{White_2011,Bonocore_2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Furthermore, the values of $\tilde{\alpha}$ below and above unity correlate with the inferred absence, or presence of abundant dark matter in the astrophysical systems.
elements.sent-2411-207: Hence, it is reasonable that a nonlocal EFT of GR might impact dark matter estimates in cosmological and astrophysical settings.
elements.sent-2411-208: Our considerations in this paper are very generic, anticipating the construction of a novel EFT of GR.
elements.sent-2411-209: At the moment, we do not have an effective theory in $\tilde{\alpha}$ which can be used to actually calculate corrections to the post-Newtonian expansion.
elements.sent-2411-210: However, we can discuss where such effective theory corrections might become necessary.
elements.sent-2411-211: In particular, the Bullet cluster \cite{Clowe_2004 , also fits in the range of validity of the approach here, since it is a collision with significant velocities and an impact parameter comparable to the radius of curvature of the galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-212: The calculations here do not touch the issue of light-bending in relativity, but it is reasonable that a nonlocal expansion around a scale such as $\tilde{\alpha}$ could yield significant corrections to the lensing observables used to characterise the dark matter distribution within galaxy clusters \cite{Clowe_2004,Bartelmann_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Most importantly, the relevance of the suggested nonlocal expansion of GR, and its relation to dark matter estimates, can be directly tested with the upcoming optical surveys, i.e., Euclid, DESI and LSST.
elements.sent-2411-214: Since \eqref{scaling is proposed as a scaling observable, its relation to the dark matter-to-baryon content can be verified using detailed galaxy surveys that probe the necessary astrophysical quantities.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Particularly, we note that ultra-faint dwarf galaxies \cite{Simon_2019 would constitute an ideal probe as their large morphological variance (within a well-defined low mass range) will allow upcoming surveys to gauge a wide range of the observables of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-216: Indeed, galaxies with large $\ave{R}$ but smaller $\ave{J ,\ave{V}$ should exhibit a smaller dark matter fraction than galaxies with similar curvature but larger $\ave{J ,\ave{V}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-217: With a sample with a broad distribution in $\ave{R ,\ave{J ,$ and $\ave{V}$ separately, observations should distinguish between a robust scaling (that would provide evidence that a novel, nonlocal EFT of GR wold be relevant for dark matter estimates) from a large spread (which would prove that dark matter estimates are unrelated to the expansion given here).}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Finally, one piece of evidence of dark matter where angular momentum seems to play no role is based on the anisotropies of the CMB \cite{Planck_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-219: However, if the evidence of magnetic fields on such scales \cite{Matarrese_2005,Durrer_2013 is vindicated, independently of their origin, an angular momentum associated with them is inevitable, and a non-local general relativistic effect arising from this angular momentum could be non-negligible.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Moreover, the work by Bruni and collaborators \cite{Bruni_2013,Thomas_2015 gives the first tantalising evidence of the importance that angular momentum and frame-dragging might have even in cosmology at the level of structure formation.}
elements.sent-2411-221: In conclusion, driven by analogies with non-Abelian gauge theory, we have argued that post-Newtonian effective field theory might fail when the proposed adimensional constant $\tilde{\alpha}$ is non-negligible.
elements.sent-2411-222: We point out that potentially $\tilde{\alpha}$ could be non-negligible for other setups, such as the Bullet cluster and cosmological scales, where dark matter dominates the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-223: Therefore, the future development of a nonlinear many-body effective theory of general relativity suitable for large $\tilde{\alpha}$ represents an interesting challenge.
elements.sent-2411-224: Its precise role in dark matter estimates, and thus its relevance in modern cosmology, could be ascertained with a survey of galaxy properties, particularly ultra-faint dwarf galaxies, by verifying if the perceived dark matter component scales with the estimate of $\tilde{\alpha}$ from observations.. \acknowledgments M.G.
